title: Calculus I
subject: math
summary: Foundations through derivatives from first principles and the standard differentiation rules.

modules.title: Foundations
modules.summary: Refresh function notation and value lookup before building the derivative. Read each input, find one output, and keep the parentheses honest.

lessons.title: Functions Refresher
lessons.summary: Read a function as input goes in, one output comes out. Evaluate f(x) at a number and at x + h.

objectives.text: State that a function maps each input to exactly one output.
Read function notation f(x) as the output produced by input x.
Evaluate f(x) at a single number and read the value from a graph.
Evaluate f(x + h) by substituting the whole expression into f.
Avoid dropping parentheses when the input is an expression.

blocks.title: Functions Refresher
blocks.subtitle: Each input goes in. Exactly one output comes out. Keep the parentheses on every substitution.

objectives.value: as a rule that sends each input to one output.
Check the common mistake: dropping parentheses around the input expression.

body.value: . The set of allowed inputs is the
. The set of produced outputs is the

keyIdeas.value: Same input always gives the same output.
Different inputs are allowed to share an output, but one input cannot have two outputs.

blocks.caption: Read this as: the function f sends input x to the output x squared.

body.value: Pick any input first. Apply the rule. The output is fixed. Pick the same input again and the output is the same number.

takeaway.value: Function evaluation is a lookup. Use
to keep the input and the output visible together.

blocks.title: Compare: read values from y = x^2
blocks.description: Notice the curve y = x^2 first. Two marked points read the output at x = 1 and at x = 3. The point at x = 1 reads y = 1. The point at x = 3 reads y = 9.

annotations.label: f(1) = 1
annotations.text: Read up from x = 1 to the curve. The output is 1.

annotations.label: f(3) = 9
annotations.text: Read up from x = 3 to the curve. The output is 9.

body.value: Read the graph in this order: pick an input on the x axis, move up to the curve, then read the output on the y axis. The notation
says the same thing in symbols.

takeaway.value: The graph and the notation describe one rule. The notation is the form used in algebra below.

blocks.title: Evaluate f(x + h) for f(x) = x^2
blocks.goal: Substitute the whole expression x + h into f without dropping parentheses.

steps.label: Write the rule
steps.explanation: State the function before any substitution.

steps.label: Substitute the whole input
steps.explanation: Replace x with the expression x + h. Keep the parentheses around x + h.

steps.label: Expand the square
steps.explanation: Apply the square to every term inside the parentheses. The cross term is 2xh.

steps.label: State the result
steps.explanation: This is the value of f at the input x + h. It is the form used in the difference quotient.

finalAnswer.summary: Substituting x + h into x^2 and expanding leaves x^2, the cross term 2xh, and h^2.

blocks.interpretation: The expression x + h is the next input the derivative lesson will use. Keep the parentheses until after the square is expanded.

blocks.title: Dropping the parentheses around x + h
blocks.misconception: Substituting x + h into f(x) = x^2 means writing x + h^2.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing f(x + h) = x + h^2 instead of f(x + h) = (x + h)^2.
blocks.whyWrong: The square applies to the whole input x + h. Without parentheses, only h is squared, which changes the output.
blocks.correction: Substitute the whole expression in parentheses: f(x + h) = (x + h)^2. Then expand to x^2 + 2xh + h^2.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you keep parentheses around the whole input before applying the rule?

body.value: Before the quiz, say it in words. To evaluate
with the whole expression in parentheses, then apply the rule.

takeaway.value: Same rule, new input. The parentheses keep the rule honest.

options.feedback: Correct. Square 3 to get 9, then subtract 1.
Review the rule. The function subtracts 1 after squaring.
Review the order. Square 3 first, then subtract 1.
Review the substitution. Replace x with 3 before subtracting 1.

questions.hint: Replace x with 3 in the rule, then simplify.

feedback.correct: Correct. Substitute x + h for x, then distribute the 2.
feedback.incorrect: Review the parentheses mistake. Substitute (x + h) into 2x + 1, then distribute the 2 to get 2x + 2h + 1.

questions.hint: Replace x with x + h, then distribute.

items.value: sends each input to exactly one output.
as the output the rule produces from the input
To evaluate at an expression, substitute the whole expression in parentheses, then simplify.

keyIdeas: A function sends each input to exactly one output.
Function notation f(x) names the output produced by the input x.
To evaluate f at an expression, substitute the whole expression in parentheses.
For f(x) = x^2, f(x + h) = x^2 + 2xh + h^2.
recallPrompts: What does same input, same output mean for a function?
Why must x + h stay inside parentheses when substituting into x^2?
What is f(3) when f(x) = x^2 - 1, and why?
What is f(x + h) when f(x) = 2x + 1, written without parentheses?

lessons.title: Limits Intuitively
lessons.summary: Build the limit idea by watching what value a function approaches as the input approaches a target from both sides.

objectives.text: Read lim_{x→a} f(x) = L as the value f approaches as the input approaches a.
Estimate a limit by sampling inputs close to the target from the left and from the right.
Recognise that a limit can exist even when the function is undefined at the target input.
Distinguish the limit value as x approaches a from the function value f(a).
Avoid the shortcut of substituting x = a before checking that the function is defined there.

blocks.title: Limits Intuitively
blocks.subtitle: Watch the output as the input slides toward a target. Approach from both sides.

objectives.value: value with the function value at the target.

blocks.title: Observe: the output as x approaches a

body.value: on the x axis and pick inputs that move toward it. A
gets close to as the input approaches
. The input never has to land exactly on
for the limit to make sense.

keyIdeas.value: The limit is about the trend, not about plugging in

blocks.caption: Read aloud: the limit of f(x) as x approaches a equals L.

blocks.title: Read: the symbols match the picture

body.value: says the input is moving toward
produces outputs along the way; the limit
is the value those outputs head toward.

takeaway.value: If both sides head to the same number, that number is the limit. If the two sides disagree, the limit does not exist.

blocks.title: Approaching x = 2 on f(x) = 2x + 1
blocks.description: Notice the line y = 2x + 1 first. Marked points at x = 1.5, 1.9, 2.1, and 2.5 show inputs sliding toward 2 from both sides. The outputs are 4, 4.8, 5.2, and 6, heading toward 5.

annotations.label: limit = 5
annotations.text: Both sides head toward output 5 as the input approaches 2.

blocks.title: Estimate lim_{x→2}(2x + 1)
blocks.goal: Estimate the limit by sampling inputs that approach 2 from both sides.

steps.label: Sample inputs from the left
steps.explanation: Choose inputs less than 2 that move closer to 2.

steps.label: Evaluate from the left
steps.explanation: Compute the outputs at the chosen left-side inputs.

steps.label: Sample inputs from the right
steps.explanation: Choose inputs greater than 2 that move closer to 2.

steps.label: Evaluate from the right
steps.explanation: Compute the outputs at the chosen right-side inputs.

steps.label: Compare the two trends
steps.explanation: Both sides head toward 5; the gap to 5 shrinks at every step.

steps.label: State the limit
steps.explanation: Because both sides agree on 5, the limit equals 5.

finalAnswer.summary: The outputs head to 5 from both sides, so the limit is 5.

blocks.interpretation: For this line, the limit equals f(2) = 5. That match is a special case, not a definition.

blocks.title: Estimate lim_{x→2}(x^2 − 4)/(x − 2)
blocks.goal: Estimate the limit when the function is undefined at the target input.

steps.label: Check the function at x = 2
steps.explanation: Substitute x = 2 first to see what happens at the target itself.

steps.label: Sample inputs near 2
steps.explanation: The limit only requires inputs close to 2, not equal to 2.

steps.label: Simplify while x ≠ 2
steps.explanation: Factor the numerator and cancel the common (x − 2) since x is not 2 in our samples.

steps.label: Read the simplified form near 2
steps.explanation: On the simplified rule x + 2, the output heads to 4 as x heads to 2.

steps.label: State the limit
steps.explanation: The limit is 4 even though g is undefined exactly at x = 2.

finalAnswer.summary: The function value at 2 is undefined, but the outputs near 2 head to 4.

blocks.interpretation: The limit answers a trend question. A missing value at the target does not block the trend.

blocks.title: g(x) = (x^2 − 4)/(x − 2) approaches 4 at x = 2
blocks.description: Notice the line y = x + 2 first, since (x^2 − 4)/(x − 2) simplifies to x + 2 when x is not 2. At x = 2 the function is undefined: the point (2, 4) is marked as the limit value the outputs head toward from both sides.

annotations.label: g undefined at x = 2
annotations.text: The function is undefined at x = 2, but the outputs from both sides head to 4.

blocks.title: Plugging in x = a to find the limit
blocks.misconception: The limit of f(x) as x approaches a is always f(a).
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing lim_{x→2} (x^2 − 4)/(x − 2) = (4 − 4)/(2 − 2) = 0/0, then concluding the limit does not exist.
blocks.whyWrong: The limit asks about the trend as x approaches the target, not the value at the target. The form 0/0 is a signal to simplify, not a final answer.
blocks.correction: Sample inputs near 2, or simplify (x^2 − 4)/(x − 2) to x + 2 while x ≠ 2, then read the trend: outputs head to 4.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you ask what the outputs do near a, or did you only check the value at a?

blocks.title: Treating an undefined value as no limit
blocks.misconception: If the function is undefined at x = a, the limit cannot exist.
blocks.incorrectStep: Stopping at g(2) is undefined and writing lim_{x→2} g(x) does not exist.
blocks.whyWrong: Existence of the limit depends on the trend from both sides, not on whether f(a) is defined.
blocks.correction: Sample inputs close to 2 from both sides. If both sides head to the same number, that number is the limit.
blocks.checkPrompt: Are both sides agreeing on one value?

body.value: Before the quiz, predict the trend. For

takeaway.value: If your left-side and right-side samples agree, you have the

options.feedback: Correct. Inputs near 3 produce outputs near 2(3) + 1 = 7.
3 is the target input, not the output. Read the output the function produces as x approaches 3.
1 is f(0), not the limit near x = 3.

questions.hint: Sample 2.9 and 3.1 and check which value the outputs hug.

options.feedback: Correct. The simplified rule x + 2 heads to 4 as x heads to 2, even though g(2) is undefined.
Review the second mistake block. The limit measures the trend, not the value at the target.
0/0 is a signal to simplify, not the final value of the limit.

questions.hint: Factor x^2 − 4 and cancel the common factor while x ≠ 2.

feedback.correct: Correct. Inputs near 0 produce outputs near 5 from both sides.
feedback.incorrect: Sample x = −0.1 and 0.1; both outputs are very close to 5.

questions.hint: Pick one input slightly less than 0 and one slightly greater than 0.

items.value: Estimate the limit by sampling inputs that approach the target from both sides.
If the two sides agree on a value, that value is the limit.
A limit can exist even when the
is undefined at the target input.

keyIdeas: A limit names the value f(x) approaches as x approaches a target.
Sample inputs from both sides of the target.
If the two sides agree, the limit equals that value.
A limit can exist even when f(a) is undefined.
recallPrompts: What does lim_{x→a} f(x) = L say in words?
Why do we sample inputs from both sides of the target?
Why does lim_{x→2} (x^2 − 4)/(x − 2) equal 4 even though g(2) is undefined?
What does 0/0 signal when you try to plug x = a directly?

lessons.title: One-Sided and Infinite Limits
lessons.summary: Split the limit into a left side and a right side. Read infinite limits as a trend, not a value to plug in.

objectives.text: Read lim_{x→a^-} f(x) and lim_{x→a^+} f(x) aloud as left- and right-sided limits.
Decide whether the two-sided limit exists by comparing the one-sided limits.
Recognise an infinite limit when outputs grow without bound near a vertical asymptote.
Distinguish a jump (no limit) from an infinite limit (a trend toward ±∞).
Avoid treating ∞ as a number that can be substituted for the input.

sections.title: Split the Approach into Two Sides

blocks.title: One-Sided and Infinite Limits
blocks.subtitle: Approach the target from one side at a time. Read ∞ as a trend, not a number to plug in.

objectives.value: from the left and from the right separately.

blocks.title: Observe: approach the target from only one side

body.value: only watches inputs that lie on one side of the target. The left-sided limit
exists when both one-sided limits exist and agree.

keyIdeas.value: The two-sided limit exists exactly when the left- and right-sided limits exist and agree.
If the two sides disagree on real numbers, the

blocks.caption: Read aloud: left-sided limit equals L-minus; right-sided limit equals L-plus.

blocks.title: Read: the small superscript chooses a side

takeaway.value: When the two sides agree, the two-sided limit equals that shared value. When the two sides disagree, the two-sided limit does not exist.

blocks.title: f(x) = x/|x| near x = 0
blocks.description: Notice two flat pieces. The function equals -1 for x < 0 and +1 for x > 0, and is undefined at 0. The left-sided limit at 0 is -1; the right-sided limit at 0 is +1. The two sides disagree, so the two-sided limit does not exist.

annotations.label: Jump at x = 0
annotations.text: Left side approaches -1; right side approaches +1. The function jumps by 2 at x = 0, so the two-sided limit does not exist.

sections.title: Read Trends That Grow Without Bound

blocks.title: One-sided limits of x/|x| at x = 0
blocks.goal: Compute both one-sided limits at 0 and decide whether the two-sided limit exists.
blocks.given: f(x) = x/|x| for x ≠ 0, target a = 0

steps.label: Restate the rule by sign
steps.explanation: When x is negative, |x| = -x, so x/|x| = x/(-x) = -1. When x is positive, |x| = x, so x/|x| = x/x = 1.

steps.label: Sample inputs from the left
steps.explanation: Pick inputs less than 0 that move toward 0. Every left input gives the output -1.

steps.label: State the left-sided limit
steps.explanation: Every left-side sample is -1, so the left-sided limit equals -1.

steps.label: Sample inputs from the right
steps.explanation: Pick inputs greater than 0 that move toward 0. Every right input gives the output 1.

steps.label: State the right-sided limit
steps.explanation: Every right-side sample is 1, so the right-sided limit equals 1.

steps.label: Compare the two sides
steps.explanation: The left side heads to -1; the right side heads to 1. They disagree, so the two-sided limit does not exist.

finalAnswer.summary: Both one-sided limits exist but disagree, so the two-sided limit does not exist.

blocks.interpretation: Each one-sided limit can exist on its own. Only when both exist and agree do we say the two-sided limit exists.

blocks.title: Recognise lim_{x→0} 1/x^2 as an infinite limit
blocks.goal: Describe the behaviour of 1/x^2 as x approaches 0 from both sides.

steps.label: Check the function at x = 0
steps.explanation: Substituting x = 0 makes the denominator zero, so h(0) is undefined.

steps.label: Sample inputs from the left
steps.explanation: Pick inputs less than 0 that move toward 0. The outputs climb because the denominator gets tiny and positive.

steps.label: Name the left-side trend
steps.explanation: The outputs grow without bound, heading to +∞.

steps.label: Sample inputs from the right
steps.explanation: Pick inputs greater than 0 that move toward 0. Squaring removes the sign, so the outputs climb the same way.

steps.label: Name the right-side trend
steps.explanation: Both sides head to +∞ because x^2 is always positive.

steps.label: Write the two-sided infinite limit
steps.explanation: Both sides agree on +∞, so we write the two-sided limit as +∞. The value +∞ is a trend, not a real number.

finalAnswer.summary: Outputs grow without bound from both sides, so the limit is +∞ (a trend, not a number).

blocks.interpretation: Writing the limit as +∞ records the trend. It does not mean we plugged x = 0 into the formula.

blocks.title: h(x) = 1/x^2 near x = 0
blocks.description: Notice two steep branches rising on each side of x = 0. As x approaches 0 from the left or from the right, h(x) climbs without bound. The y-axis is clipped at 12 to keep the rest of the picture readable.

annotations.label: Vertical asymptote x = 0
annotations.text: Outputs grow without bound from both sides as x approaches 0. The line x = 0 is a vertical asymptote.

blocks.title: Claiming a two-sided limit when the sides disagree
blocks.misconception: If both one-sided limits exist, the two-sided limit exists too.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing lim_{x→0} x/|x| = -1 (or = 1) just because each side gives a clean number.
blocks.whyWrong: The two-sided limit only exists when the one-sided limits agree on the same value. -1 ≠ 1, so the two-sided limit does not exist.
blocks.correction: When the one-sided limits disagree, report each one separately and write 'the two-sided limit does not exist.'
blocks.checkPrompt: Are the left-sided and right-sided limits the same number?

blocks.title: Treating ∞ as a number you can plug in
blocks.misconception: Saying lim_{x→0} 1/x^2 = +∞ means plugging x = 0 to get 1/0 = ∞.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing 1/0^2 = 1/0 = ∞ and treating ∞ as the function value at 0.
blocks.whyWrong: Division by zero is undefined; ∞ is not a real number. The notation lim = +∞ records that outputs grow without bound, not a value reached by substitution.
blocks.correction: Read lim_{x→0} 1/x^2 = +∞ as 'the outputs grow without bound as x approaches 0'. Confirm by sampling inputs that approach 0.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did the outputs settle near a real number, or did they keep growing without bound?

body.value: Before the quiz, predict each side. For
, does the left side head toward

takeaway.value: When each one-sided trend is named honestly, you can read a
without claiming ∞ is a number.

text.value: The two-sided limit does not exist.

options.feedback: Correct. The left-sided limit is -1 and the right-sided limit is +1, so the two-sided limit does not exist.
1 is the right-sided limit only. The two-sided limit requires both sides to agree.
-1 is the left-sided limit only. The two-sided limit requires both sides to agree.
0 is the midpoint of -1 and 1, not the limit. Neither side ever heads to 0.

questions.hint: Compute the left-sided and right-sided limits separately, then compare them.

options.feedback: Correct. Both sides grow without bound because squaring forces a positive denominator.

text.value: The limit does not exist because 1/0 is undefined.

options.feedback: 1/0 is undefined as a number, but the trend on both sides is +∞. We record the trend with the infinite-limit notation.
0 is the y-value far from x = 0, not the trend near x = 0. Sample inputs close to 0 instead.
1 is h(1) and h(-1), not the trend as x approaches 0.

questions.hint: Sample x = 0.1 and x = -0.1, then x = 0.01 and x = -0.01. Watch the outputs grow.

prompt.value: by sampling inputs slightly less than 2.

feedback.correct: Correct. Sampling x = 1.9, 1.99, 1.999 gives -10, -100, -1000, heading to -∞.
feedback.incorrect: Sample x = 1.99 (denominator is -0.01, output -100) and x = 1.999 (output -1000). The outputs grow toward -∞.

questions.hint: Inputs slightly less than 2 make x − 2 a tiny negative number, so 1/(x − 2) is a large negative number.

items.value: only watches inputs on one side of the target.
exists exactly when both one-sided limits exist and agree.
records outputs growing without bound near a
; ∞ names a trend, not a value.
is the case where the one-sided limits exist but disagree.

keyIdeas: Left- and right-sided limits read each side of the target separately.
The two-sided limit exists iff the one-sided limits exist and agree.
An infinite limit records a trend toward ±∞; ∞ is not a number.
A jump discontinuity is one-sided limits that exist but disagree.
recallPrompts: Write lim_{x→a^-} f(x) and lim_{x→a^+} f(x) in words.
What is the existence test for the two-sided limit at x = a?
Why does lim_{x→0} 1/x^2 = +∞ even though h(0) is undefined?
How does a jump discontinuity differ from an infinite limit?

modules.title: Derivatives from First Principles
modules.summary: Build the derivative as a limit of slopes between two points. Stay inside the definition before invoking shortcuts.

lessons.title: Derivative as a Limit
lessons.summary: Build the derivative from slopes between two points. Keep h nonzero until the limit step.

objectives.text: Observe average slope between two different inputs.
Use the difference quotient with h not equal to 0 before the limit is taken.
Explain the derivative as the value approached by those slopes.
Compute the derivative of f(x) = x^2 from first principles.
Avoid substituting h = 0 before the quotient is simplified.

blocks.title: Derivatives from First Principles
blocks.subtitle: Start with two different points. Then ask what slope they approach as the gap shrinks.

objectives.value: Observe a slope between two points first.

keyIdeas.value: The two inputs must be different, so
asks what value these slopes approach when the inputs move together.

body.value: for the nearby input. The value
is the horizontal gap, and during algebra

takeaway.value: Calculate while
. Use the
only after the quotient no longer divides by

blocks.caption: Formal rule. Compare f(x+h) to f(x), divide by the nonzero gap h, then let h approach 0.

blocks.title: Secant line approaching tangent slope
blocks.description: Notice the two marked points first. The secant line uses both points. If the second point moves toward x = 1, the secant slope approaches the tangent slope 2 at the fixed point.

annotations.label: x = 1
annotations.text: The tangent slope here is 2.

blocks.title: Shrinking the gap toward the tangent slope
blocks.description: Notice the fixed base point (1, 1) first. As h shrinks from 2 to 1 to 0.5, the secant rotates toward the tangent line at (1, 1). The secant slopes 4, 3, and 2.5 approach the tangent slope 2.

annotations.text: Base point stays fixed while h shrinks toward 0.

annotations.label: slopes 4, 3, 2.5 toward 2
annotations.text: Secant slopes for h = 2, 1, 0.5 are 4, 3, 2.5; they approach the tangent slope 2.

body.value: Read the graph in this order: the curve, the fixed point, the nearby point, then the
. The quotient measures the slope of that secant line.

takeaway.value: The graph is a check on the formula
. It does not replace the algebra.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = x^2
blocks.goal: Calculate f'(x) by simplifying before the limit is evaluated.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Start with the formal rule. The gap h is not zero yet.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = x^2
steps.explanation: Replace f(x+h) with (x+h)^2. Replace f(x) with x^2.

steps.label: Expand the square
steps.explanation: Write out the numerator so the matching terms are visible.

steps.label: Combine like terms
steps.explanation: The terms x^2 and -x^2 add to zero.

steps.label: Factor h while h is not zero
steps.explanation: Each remaining numerator term has a factor h. This is the safe reason cancellation will work.

steps.label: Cancel the common factor
steps.explanation: Now the quotient no longer divides by h.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: Now h can approach 0. There is no division by h left.

finalAnswer.summary: The tangent slope of y = x^2 at input x equals 2x.

blocks.interpretation: The result says: at input x, the tangent slope of y = x^2 is 2x.

blocks.title: f(x) = x^2 and its derivative f'(x) = 2x
blocks.description: Notice the curve y = x^2 first, then the straight line y = 2x. The derivative line reads the tangent slope at each input: at x = 1 the slope is 2, at x = 2 the slope is 4, at x = 3 the slope is 6.

annotations.label: f'(1) = 2
annotations.text: On the derivative line, x = 1 reads y = 2. The tangent slope at x = 1 is 2.

annotations.label: f'(2) = 4
annotations.text: Both the curve and the derivative line read 4 at x = 2. The tangent slope at x = 2 is 4.

annotations.label: f'(3) = 6
annotations.text: At x = 3 the curve climbs to 9, but the derivative line gives 6. The tangent slope at x = 3 is 6.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = 3x - 5
blocks.goal: Use the same definition for a linear function.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Use the same first-principles rule. Keep h nonzero during algebra.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = 3x - 5
steps.explanation: Replace f(x+h) with 3(x+h)-5. Subtract the whole expression 3x-5.

steps.label: Expand and distribute the subtraction
steps.explanation: Expand 3(x+h). Then apply the minus sign to every term in 3x-5.

steps.label: Combine like terms
steps.explanation: The x terms cancel. The constants also cancel.

steps.label: Cancel h while h is not zero
steps.explanation: The numerator and denominator share the factor h. Cancel it before the limit step.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: The expression is already the constant 3.

finalAnswer.summary: Every input on y = 3x - 5 has tangent slope 3.

blocks.interpretation: The result says: every point on y = 3x - 5 has tangent slope 3.

blocks.title: Forgetting to subtract the whole function
blocks.whyWrong: The entire value f(x) is being subtracted. The minus sign must apply to both 3x and -5.
blocks.correction: Keep f(x) in parentheses when substituting, then distribute the subtraction: -(3x-5) = -3x + 5.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you subtract every term in f(x), including the constant term?

blocks.title: Substituting h = 0 too early
blocks.misconception: The limit h -> 0 means h can be replaced with 0 immediately.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing ((x + 0)^2 - x^2) / 0 at the start of the calculation.
blocks.whyWrong: That creates division by zero before the expression has been simplified.
blocks.correction: Keep h nonzero while simplifying, cancel the common factor h, then evaluate the limit.
blocks.checkPrompt: Can the expression still contain division by h when you substitute h = 0?

body.value: Observe the quotient before the quiz. If the denominator is still

takeaway.value: Say the path out loud: factor
, cancel while
, then take the limit.

prompt.value: What must happen before you evaluate the limit for

options.feedback: Correct. This removes division by h before h approaches 0.
Review the mistake block. Substituting h = 0 first creates division by zero.

text.value: Cancel the h in the denominator without factoring the numerator.

options.feedback: Review the factoring step. You can cancel h only after it is a common factor of the whole numerator and denominator.

questions.hint: Look for the step that removes division by h without pretending h already equals 0.

prompt.value: , what constant slope should the first-principles calculation give for

feedback.correct: Correct. The quotient simplifies to 3 before the limit is evaluated.
feedback.incorrect: Review the parentheses mistake. After distributing the subtraction, the quotient becomes 3h/h while h is nonzero, so the limit is 3.

questions.hint: After simplifying, the expression no longer depends on x or h.

items.value: starts with slopes between two different inputs.
. It means compare, divide by the nonzero gap, then take the limit.
, first principles give the constant slope

keyIdeas: The difference quotient compares two points over a nonzero gap h.
The limit describes the slope value approached as h moves toward 0.
For f(x) = x^2, first-principles algebra gives f'(x) = 2x.
For f(x) = 3x - 5, first-principles algebra gives the constant slope f'(x) = 3.
recallPrompts: What should you observe before doing any algebra?
Why must h stay nonzero before the limit is evaluated?
Where does the factor h come from in the x^2 calculation?
Why must f(x) be subtracted as the whole expression in the linear-function calculation?

lessons.title: Derivative at a Point
lessons.summary: Keep one input fixed and calculate one tangent slope.

objectives.text: Compare a derivative function with one derivative value.
Use the point form of the first-principles definition with a fixed input.
Compute f'(2) for f(x) = x^2 from first principles.
Interpret the result as the tangent slope at (2, 4).
Avoid mixing the variable input x with the fixed input.

blocks.title: Derivative at a Point
blocks.subtitle: Keep one point fixed. Let the nearby point move toward it.

body.value: rule for variable inputs. A derivative value such as

keyIdeas.value: The nearby input is the base point plus a nonzero gap

body.value: is nonzero before the limit is taken.

takeaway.value: The input
is fixed. Only the gap

blocks.caption: Point form. The input a is fixed before the limit is taken.

blocks.title: Tangent slope at x = 2
blocks.description: Notice the fixed point (2, 4). The secant through (2, 4) and (3, 9) has slope 5. The tangent line at (2, 4) has slope 4, which is the value the secant slopes approach.

annotations.text: The tangent slope here is 4.
This nearby point gives the h = 1 secant.

blocks.title: Secants approach the tangent from both sides
blocks.description: Notice the fixed point (2, 4) first. The secant from x = 1 has slope 3. The secant to x = 3 has slope 5. As the nearby input moves toward 2 from either side, the secant slope approaches the tangent slope 4.

annotations.text: Left secant slope 3 and right secant slope 5 both close in on the tangent slope 4.

body.value: Read the graph in this order: the fixed point, the nearby point, the

takeaway.value: The graph checks the calculation
. It does not change which input is fixed.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = x^2 at x = 2
blocks.goal: Calculate f'(2) without using a variable base input.

steps.label: Write the point definition
steps.explanation: Fix the base input at 2. Let the nearby input be 2 + h.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = x^2
steps.explanation: Replace f(2+h) with (2+h)^2. Replace f(2) with 2^2.

steps.label: Expand the square
steps.explanation: Expand (2+h)^2 so the matching constants are visible.

steps.label: Combine like terms
steps.explanation: The constants 4 and -4 add to zero.

steps.label: Factor h while h is not zero
steps.explanation: Each remaining numerator term has a factor h. The gap is still nonzero.

steps.label: Cancel the common factor
steps.explanation: Now the expression no longer divides by h.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: Now h can approach 0 without division by zero.

finalAnswer.summary: At the point (2, 4), the tangent slope of y = x^2 is 4.

blocks.interpretation: The result says: at the point (2, 4), the tangent slope of y = x^2 is 4.

blocks.title: Read f'(2) from the derivative line
blocks.description: Notice the derivative line y = 2x first. Reading off x = 2 gives y = 4. That value is the tangent slope of y = x^2 at the fixed point (2, 4).

annotations.label: f'(2) = 4
annotations.text: On the derivative line y = 2x, the input x = 2 reads y = 4. That is the tangent slope at the fixed point (2, 4).

blocks.title: Mixing the fixed input with the variable input
blocks.misconception: When computing f'(2), the numerator can still use the variable x.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing ((x+h)^2-2^2)/h when the target is f'(2).
blocks.whyWrong: The base input has already been fixed at 2. Both function values must use 2 and 2 + h.
blocks.correction: Use ((2+h)^2-2^2)/h, simplify while h is nonzero, and then take the limit.
blocks.checkPrompt: Have you replaced every base input with 2 before simplifying?

body.value: Observe the target before the quiz.
means the base input is already

takeaway.value: Use
and
. Do not bring
back into the quotient.

options.feedback: Correct. The base input is fixed at 2, and the nearby input is 2 + h.
Review the mistake block. That mixes the variable input x with the fixed input 2.
Review the limit step. Substituting h = 0 before simplifying creates division by zero.

questions.hint: Keep one point at x = 2. Move the other point to x = 2 + h.

feedback.correct: Correct. The point difference quotient simplifies to 4 + h, so the limit is 4.
feedback.incorrect: Review the fixed-input substitution. The quotient must simplify to 4 + h before the limit is evaluated.

questions.hint: The tangent slope at x = 2 is the value approached by the secant slopes.

items.value: value fixes one input before the limit is taken.
, first principles give the one value

keyIdeas: A derivative value such as f'(2) fixes the base input before taking the limit.
The nearby input is 2 + h, where h is nonzero before the limit is evaluated.
For f(x) = x^2, first principles give f'(2) = 4.
The value f'(2) is the tangent slope at the point (2, 4).
recallPrompts: What is fixed before the calculation starts?
What is the difference between f'(x) and f'(2)?
Why does the quotient use f(2+h)-f(2) instead of f(x+h)-f(2)?
Where is h still required to be nonzero in the point calculation?

lessons.title: Constant Function Derivative
lessons.summary: Use first principles to see why a function with no output change has derivative 0.

objectives.text: Observe a function whose output stays the same.
Use the difference quotient for f(x) = 7 while h is not equal to 0.
Explain why the numerator becomes 0.
Compute f'(x) = 0 for a constant function.
Avoid treating the constant output as a slope.

blocks.title: Constant Function Derivative
blocks.subtitle: When the output does not change, the average slope is zero before the limit step.

objectives.value: Observe two inputs with the same output.
Check the mistake: copying the output value as the slope.

blocks.title: Observe: same output at both inputs

body.value: measures output change, so it becomes

blocks.caption: Constant-function setup. The numerator is zero because both function values are 7.

blocks.title: Flat function has zero slope
blocks.description: Notice the two marked points first. Their outputs have the same height, so the secant line is horizontal and its slope is 0.

annotations.label: same output
annotations.text: The output change from x = 0 to x = 2 is 0.

blocks.title: Three constants, all with slope 0
blocks.description: Notice that y = 3, y = 5, and y = 7 are all horizontal lines. The vertical position changes with the constant, but the output never changes with x. Every secant slope is 0, so every tangent slope is 0.

annotations.label: every slope is 0
annotations.text: Each constant value sets a height. The output change is 0 between any two inputs, so every tangent slope is 0.

body.value: Read the graph in this order: the flat line, the two marked points, then the horizontal
. The formula sees the same fact as

takeaway.value: The graph checks the numerator
. Keep
until the quotient is simplified.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = 7
blocks.goal: Calculate f'(x) for a function whose output never changes.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Use the same first-principles rule. The input gap h is not zero yet.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = 7
steps.explanation: Both f(x+h) and f(x) equal 7.

steps.label: Combine the output change
steps.explanation: The numerator is zero because the output did not change.

steps.label: Simplify while h is not zero
steps.explanation: Zero divided by any nonzero h is zero.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: The expression is already the constant 0.

finalAnswer.summary: Every input on the flat function y = 7 has tangent slope 0.

blocks.interpretation: The result says: a flat function has tangent slope 0 at every input.

blocks.title: f(x) = 7 and its derivative f'(x) = 0
blocks.description: Notice the flat function y = 7 sitting at height 7. The derivative function y = 0 sits on the x-axis. Both lines are horizontal, because the slope of any constant is 0 at every input.

annotations.label: f'(x) = 0
annotations.text: The derivative line never leaves the x-axis. Every input gives slope 0.

blocks.title: Treating the constant output as the slope
blocks.misconception: The derivative of f(x) = 7 should be 7 because the output is always 7.
blocks.whyWrong: The derivative measures output change per input change. The output value is 7, but the output change is 0.
blocks.correction: Use f(x+h)-f(x)=7-7=0, then the quotient is 0 while h is nonzero.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you compare two outputs, or did you copy the output value?

body.value: Observe the numerator before the quiz. For

takeaway.value: If the two outputs are equal, the numerator is

options.feedback: Correct. The output does not change, so the numerator is 0.
Review the mistake block. The output value is 7, but the output change is 0.
The value h is the input gap. The numerator asks for output change.

questions.hint: Compare the two output values before dividing by h.

feedback.correct: Correct. The quotient simplifies to 0 before the limit is evaluated.
feedback.incorrect: Review the numerator. A constant function has output change 0, so the derivative is 0.

questions.hint: A flat graph has zero tangent slope.

items.value: has the same output at every input.

keyIdeas: A constant function has no output change between two inputs.
For f(x) = 7, the numerator f(x+h)-f(x) becomes 0.
Zero divided by nonzero h is zero before the limit is evaluated.
A flat function has derivative 0 at every input.
recallPrompts: What stays the same in a constant function?
Why does f(x+h)-f(x) become 0?
Where is h still required to be nonzero?
Why is the derivative 0 instead of 7?

lessons.title: Differentiability vs Continuity
lessons.summary: Continuity demands no jump. Differentiability adds a single tangent slope. A corner shows the second demand can fail.

objectives.text: State the two demands: continuity needs a limit equal to the value, differentiability needs a single tangent slope.
Confirm f(x) = |x| is continuous at x = 0 using one-sided limits.
Compute the left and right difference quotients of f(x) = |x| at x = 0.
Conclude the derivative does not exist where the two one-sided difference quotients disagree.
Distinguish 'continuous means differentiable' from the correct direction 'differentiable means continuous'.

blocks.title: Differentiability vs Continuity
blocks.subtitle: A continuous graph can still have a corner where the tangent slope fails to exist.

objectives.value: Observe two demands placed on a function at the same input.
Compute the left and right difference quotients and compare them.
Name the direction: differentiable implies continuous, not the reverse.

blocks.title: Observe: two demands at the same input

body.value: Two ideas can both fail or succeed at one input. A

keyIdeas.value: Continuity says the graph does not jump at
Differentiability says the graph has one tangent slope at
Differentiable implies continuous. A continuous function may still fail to be differentiable.

blocks.caption: Continuity at x = a: the two-sided limit exists and equals the function value.

blocks.title: Predict: where could the derivative fail?

body.value: Two short questions before the graph. Ask each separately. First, does the

takeaway.value: Continuity asks about values. Differentiability asks about slopes. The two demands are not the same.

blocks.title: The corner of f(x) = |x| at x = 0
blocks.description: Notice the V-shape first. The graph reaches the point (0, 0) without a break, so the continuity demand is met. Then notice the two short secant lines: one from (-1, 1) down to (0, 0) has slope -1, and one from (0, 0) up to (1, 1) has slope +1. The slopes from the two sides disagree at the corner.

annotations.label: corner at x = 0
annotations.text: The graph reaches this point continuously, but the slope changes from -1 to +1 across it.

blocks.title: Compare: graph to the two demands

body.value: Read the graph in this order: the V-shape, the corner point at the origin, the left secant, the right secant. The corner is reached without a break, so the
demand is met. The two secants meet at the corner with different slopes, so the

takeaway.value: Continuous yes. Differentiable no. A single graph can show both answers at the same input.

blocks.title: Continuity of f(x) = |x| at x = 0
blocks.goal: Confirm that f(x) = |x| satisfies the continuity demand at x = 0.

steps.label: Read the value at x = 0
steps.explanation: The function value at the input 0 is |0| = 0.

steps.label: Approach from the left
steps.explanation: For x less than 0, |x| equals -x. As x approaches 0 from the left, -x approaches 0.

steps.label: Approach from the right
steps.explanation: For x greater than 0, |x| equals x. As x approaches 0 from the right, x approaches 0.

steps.label: Compare the two sides
steps.explanation: Both one-sided limits equal 0, so the two-sided limit is 0.

steps.label: Check the continuity demand
steps.explanation: The limit at 0 equals the value at 0, so the function is continuous at x = 0.

finalAnswer.summary: The continuity demand is met at x = 0.

blocks.interpretation: The graph passes through the corner without a break. Continuity is settled before any slope question is asked.

blocks.title: Test f'(0) for f(x) = |x|
blocks.goal: Compute the left and right difference quotients at x = 0 and compare them.

steps.label: Write the difference quotient
steps.explanation: Apply the first-principles definition at the input 0.

steps.label: Simplify for h less than 0
steps.explanation: When h is negative, |h| equals -h. The quotient becomes -h divided by h. Cancel while h is nonzero.

steps.label: State the left-side limit
steps.explanation: Every left-side value of the quotient is -1, so the left-side limit is -1.

steps.label: Simplify for h greater than 0
steps.explanation: When h is positive, |h| equals h. The quotient becomes h divided by h. Cancel while h is nonzero.

steps.label: State the right-side limit
steps.explanation: Every right-side value of the quotient is +1, so the right-side limit is +1.

steps.label: Compare the two sides
steps.explanation: The left and right limits disagree, so the two-sided limit of the difference quotient does not exist. The derivative at 0 does not exist.

finalAnswer.summary: Left slope -1 and right slope +1 disagree, so f'(0) does not exist.

blocks.interpretation: The function is continuous at 0 but is not differentiable there. The corner breaks the second demand.

blocks.title: Smooth versus cornered at x = 0
blocks.description: Notice y = x^2 first. Its slopes from the left and from the right both approach 0 at x = 0, so it has a single tangent slope there. Now notice y = |x|. Its slopes from the left and from the right disagree at the corner, so a single tangent slope is missing.

annotations.label: smooth versus corner
annotations.text: Both curves pass through (0, 0). At that input y = x^2 has matching one-sided slopes of 0, while y = |x| has slopes -1 and +1.

blocks.title: Assuming continuous means differentiable
blocks.misconception: A function with no jumps must have a derivative everywhere.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing f'(0) = 0 (or any single value) for f(x) = |x| because the graph is unbroken at x = 0.
blocks.whyWrong: Continuity only checks function values and their limit. Differentiability also asks whether the left and right difference quotients agree. At x = 0 they disagree (-1 and +1), so the derivative does not exist there.
blocks.correction: Compute the left and right difference quotients separately. Compare them. The derivative exists only when the two values agree.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you compare the two one-sided difference quotients, or did you only check the function value?

body.value: Two short questions before the quiz. First, ask whether
. Second, ask whether the same function is

takeaway.value: Answer each demand on its own. The answers can be different at the same input.

text.value: Every differentiable function is continuous, but a continuous function may fail to be differentiable.

options.feedback: Correct. Differentiable implies continuous. The reverse can fail at a corner.
Review the mistake block. f(x) = |x| is continuous at x = 0 but not differentiable there.

text.value: Continuous and differentiable mean the same thing.

options.feedback: They are different demands. Continuity is about values; differentiability is about slopes.

text.value: A function is differentiable wherever it is defined.

options.feedback: f(x) = |x| is defined at x = 0 but not differentiable there. Being defined is not enough.

questions.hint: One direction always holds. The other can fail at a corner.

options.feedback: Correct. For h greater than 0, |h|/h = h/h = 1.
That is the left-side value, where h is negative and |h| = -h.
Zero would mean the slope is flat. For h greater than 0, |h|/h equals 1.
The right-side limit exists and equals 1. Only the two-sided limit fails.

questions.hint: For h greater than 0, |h| equals h.

feedback.correct: Correct. The left and right difference quotients disagree at 0, so the derivative does not exist there.
feedback.incorrect: Review the second worked example. The left limit is -1 and the right limit is +1, so the derivative at 0 does not exist.

questions.hint: Compare the two one-sided difference quotients.

items.value: means the two one-sided difference quotients agree as
Differentiable implies continuous. The reverse is not always true.
: continuous yes, differentiable no, because the left and right limits of

keyIdeas: Continuity at x = a means lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a).
Differentiability at x = a means the left and right difference quotients agree as h → 0.
Differentiable implies continuous. The reverse can fail at a corner.
For f(x) = |x| at x = 0, the left limit is -1 and the right limit is +1, so the derivative does not exist.
recallPrompts: What does continuity at x = a require?
What does differentiability at x = a require?
Which implication always holds: continuity to differentiability, or differentiability to continuity?
Why does f(x) = |x| fail the differentiability demand at x = 0?

modules.title: Differentiation Rules
modules.summary: Derive the standard shortcut rules from first principles. Start with the power rule, then build sum, product, quotient, and chain rules.

lessons.title: The Power Rule
lessons.summary: Derive the shortcut for x^n from first principles, starting with x^2 and x^3 before generalising. Apply it to polynomials.

objectives.text: Derive d/dx(x^2) = 2x from the first-principles definition.
Derive d/dx(x^3) = 3x^2 from the first-principles definition.
State the power rule: d/dx(x^n) = n x^(n-1) for any positive integer n.
Apply the power rule to differentiate a single-term power function.
Avoid common errors: forgetting to reduce the exponent or misplacing the coefficient.

sections.title: From First Principles to a Pattern

blocks.title: The Power Rule
blocks.subtitle: Derive the shortcut for x raised to a positive integer, then apply it without the limit each time.

objectives.value: Apply the shortcut to differentiate a power

body.value: In Module B you computed the
definition. The algebra got the job done, but imagine doing that every time for
. A pattern is hiding inside the algebra. Once you see it, you can skip the limit step entirely.

blocks.title: Spot the pattern before the proof

body.value: 2 came down as a coefficient and the new exponent dropped by one. Watch whether the same thing happens for

takeaway.value: If the pattern holds: bring the
down, reduce by one.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = x² from first principles
blocks.goal: Compute f'(x) for f(x) = x² using the limit definition.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Start from the first-principles definition. h is not zero yet.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = x²
steps.explanation: Replace every f(·) with the squaring rule.

steps.label: Expand (x + h)²
steps.explanation: Use the identity (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b².

steps.label: Cancel the x² terms
steps.explanation: The x² at the start and the −x² at the end eliminate each other.

steps.label: Factor out h
steps.explanation: Every term in the numerator has a factor of h. Because h ≠ 0, divide it out.

steps.label: Take the limit as h → 0
steps.explanation: Now h may approach 0. The remaining h vanishes, leaving 2x.

finalAnswer.summary: The exponent 2 came down as a coefficient and the power dropped to 1.

blocks.interpretation: At any input x, the tangent slope of the parabola y = x² is 2x.

blocks.title: f(x) = x² and its derivative f'(x) = 2x
blocks.description: The parabola y = x² is shown in blue. Its derivative y = 2x is a straight line through the origin. At x = 1 the parabola has tangent slope 2; at x = −1 the slope is −2.

annotations.label: slope = 2
annotations.text: At x = 1, the tangent slope is 2(1) = 2.

annotations.label: slope = −2
annotations.text: At x = −1, the tangent slope is 2(−1) = −2.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = x³ from first principles
blocks.goal: Compute f'(x) for f(x) = x³ using the limit definition.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Same starting point: the first-principles limit.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = x³
steps.explanation: Replace every f(·) with the cubing rule.

steps.label: Expand (x + h)³
steps.explanation: Use (a + b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³.

steps.label: Cancel the x³ terms
steps.explanation: The leading x³ and the trailing −x³ eliminate each other.

steps.label: Factor out h
steps.explanation: Every surviving term has a factor of h. Because h ≠ 0, divide it out.

steps.label: Take the limit as h → 0
steps.explanation: Both the 3xh and h² terms vanish, leaving 3x².

finalAnswer.summary: The exponent 3 came down as a coefficient and the power dropped to 2.

blocks.interpretation: At any input x, the tangent slope of y = x³ equals 3x².

blocks.title: f(x) = x³ and its derivative f'(x) = 3x²
blocks.description: The cubic curve y = x³ rises steeply on both ends but flattens at the origin. Its derivative 3x² is a parabola that is never negative, matching the fact that the cubic's tangent slope is zero only at x = 0 and positive everywhere else.

annotations.label: slope = 0
annotations.text: At x = 0, the tangent slope is 3(0)² = 0.

annotations.label: slope = 3
annotations.text: At x = 1, the tangent slope is 3(1)² = 3.

blocks.title: The pattern across x², x³, and beyond

body.value: Compare the results so far. For a
comes down as a coefficient and the power drops by one.

takeaway.value: The pattern is: bring the exponent down, reduce by one. This is the

blocks.caption: The power rule for positive integer exponents

blocks.title: Why the pattern holds for every positive integer n

body.latex: x^n + n x^{n-1}h + (\text{terms with }h^2\text{ or higher})

keyIdeas.value: contributes exactly one term that survives the limit:

blocks.title: Differentiate f(x) = x⁵
blocks.goal: Use the power rule to find f'(x) without returning to the limit definition.

steps.label: Identify n
steps.explanation: The function is x raised to the 5th power, so n = 5.

steps.label: Bring down the exponent
steps.explanation: Multiply by n = 5.

steps.label: Reduce the exponent by 1
steps.explanation: The new exponent is 5 − 1 = 4.

finalAnswer.summary: Bring 5 down, reduce the power to 4.

blocks.interpretation: No limit work needed. The shortcut reproduces exactly what the first-principles definition would give.

blocks.title: Differentiate f(x) = x
blocks.goal: Apply the power rule to x¹ and confirm it matches the known slope of a line.

steps.label: Identify n
steps.explanation: x is the same as x¹, so n = 1.

finalAnswer.summary: The line y = x has constant slope 1 everywhere, matching intuition.

blocks.interpretation: The power rule with n = 1 gives slope 1, which matches the fact that y = x is a line with slope 1.

blocks.title: Forgetting to reduce the exponent
blocks.misconception: Bring the exponent down but leave the power unchanged.
blocks.whyWrong: The rule says n · x^(n−1). The exponent must drop by 1, giving 4x³, not 4x⁴.
blocks.correction: d/dx(x⁴) = 4x³. Always subtract 1 from the exponent after bringing it down.
blocks.checkPrompt: What is d/dx(x⁶)? Make sure both the coefficient and the new exponent are correct.

blocks.title: Swapping the coefficient and the new exponent
blocks.misconception: Write the reduced exponent as the coefficient and the original exponent minus one as the power.
blocks.incorrectStep: d/dx(x⁴) = 3x⁴  (used n−1 as the coefficient instead of n)
blocks.whyWrong: The coefficient is the original exponent n, not n − 1. The exponent that changes is the one on x.
blocks.correction: d/dx(x⁴) = 4x³. The original exponent 4 becomes the coefficient; the new exponent is 4 − 1 = 3.
blocks.checkPrompt: Differentiate x⁷. Which number becomes the coefficient, and which becomes the new power?

options.feedback: Correct. Bring the exponent 3 down and reduce the power to 2.
Not quite — you brought the exponent down but forgot to reduce the power by 1.
This uses n − 1 as the coefficient and keeps the original exponent. The coefficient should be n = 3.
The coefficient is missing — the power rule multiplies by the original exponent.

questions.hint: The power rule says d/dx(x^n) = n·x^(n−1). Plug in n = 3.

options.feedback: Correct. The exponent 6 becomes the coefficient, and the new power is 5.
The coefficient should be the original exponent 6, not 5. And the power should reduce to 5.
The coefficient is right, but the exponent must drop by 1 to give x⁵.
The power should decrease by 1, not increase.

questions.hint: Apply d/dx(x^n) = n·x^(n−1) with n = 6.

prompt.value: . Write your answer in the form ax^b.

feedback.correct: Correct. Bring the 4 down and reduce the exponent to 3.
feedback.incorrect: Apply n · x^(n−1) with n = 4. The answer is 4x³.

questions.hint: The coefficient is the original exponent, and the new exponent is one less.

items.value: down as a coefficient, then reduce the power by one.
Common error: forgetting to reduce the
or swapping the coefficient with the new power.

keyIdeas: d/dx(x^n) = n·x^(n−1) for every positive integer n.
Derivation: expand (x+h)^n by the binomial theorem; only the n·x^(n−1) term survives the limit.
For x²: exponent 2 comes down, power drops to 1, giving 2x.
For x³: exponent 3 comes down, power drops to 2, giving 3x².
Common mistake: forgetting to subtract 1 from the exponent.
recallPrompts: State the power rule for x^n.
Why does only one term from the binomial expansion survive the limit?
What is d/dx(x⁵)?
What common error do students make when applying the power rule?

lessons.title: The Sum and Difference Rules
lessons.summary: Prove that the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives, extend the idea to differences and constant multiples, and use all three rules to differentiate any polynomial.

objectives.text: State the constant multiple rule: d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f’(x).
State the sum rule: d/dx[f(x) + g(x)] = f’(x) + g’(x).
Derive the sum rule from the limit definition of the derivative.
Apply the sum, difference, and constant multiple rules to differentiate a polynomial.
Recognise that the sum and difference rules do not extend to products or quotients.

blocks.title: The Sum and Difference Rules
blocks.subtitle: Prove that derivatives pass through addition and subtraction, then combine the power rule with these rules to differentiate any polynomial.

objectives.value: by combining these rules with the

body.value: . Most functions you meet are sums of terms:
like that, you need rules that let you break the expression apart and handle each piece separately.
Imagine two bank accounts growing at different rates. One earns
dollars per day, and the other earns
dollars per day. Your total balance is
dollars per day. Rates of change add when the quantities add.

takeaway.value: If two quantities add, their rates of change add. That is the

blocks.title: Scaling a function scales its rate

body.value: If a car travels at speed
, then three identical cars still each travel at
. A constant factor passes straight through the

blocks.caption: The constant multiple rule: the constant c stays in front.

blocks.title: Differentiate f(x) = 5x³
blocks.goal: Apply the constant multiple rule with the power rule to find f’(x).

steps.label: Identify the constant factor
steps.explanation: The function is 5 times x³. The constant is c = 5 and the inner function is x³.

steps.label: Pull the constant through
steps.explanation: The constant multiple rule says the 5 stays in front of the derivative.

steps.label: Apply the power rule to x³
steps.explanation: The power rule gives d/dx(x³) = 3x². Multiply by the constant 5.

finalAnswer.summary: The constant 5 multiplied the power-rule result 3x².

blocks.interpretation: At any input x, the slope of y = 5x³ is 15x².

blocks.title: Derive the sum rule from the limit definition
blocks.goal: Show that d/dx[f(x) + g(x)] = f’(x) + g’(x) using the first-principles definition.

steps.label: Write the limit definition
steps.explanation: Let s(x) = f(x) + g(x). Start from the first-principles definition of s’(x).

steps.label: Substitute s = f + g
steps.explanation: Replace every s(·) with f(·) + g(·).

steps.label: Regroup by function
steps.explanation: Pair the f terms together and the g terms together.

steps.label: Split the fraction
steps.explanation: A sum in the numerator splits into two fractions with the same denominator.

steps.label: Apply the limit sum law
steps.explanation: The limit of a sum equals the sum of the limits, provided both limits exist.

steps.label: Recognise the derivatives
steps.explanation: Each limit is the definition of a derivative. The first is f’(x) and the second is g’(x).

finalAnswer.summary: The derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives.

blocks.interpretation: Each function contributes its own rate of change independently.

keyIdeas.value: of a difference is the difference of the derivatives.

blocks.title: f(x) = x² + x and its derivative f’(x) = 2x + 1
blocks.description: The curve y = x² + x is shown alongside its derivative y = 2x + 1. At x = 1, the tangent slope is 3. At x = −0.5, the derivative is 0 and the curve has a turning point.

annotations.label: slope = 3
annotations.text: At x = 1, f’(1) = 2(1) + 1 = 3. The slope equals the sum of the individual derivatives: 2x gives 2 and 1 gives 1.

annotations.label: slope = 0
annotations.text: At x = −0.5, f’(−0.5) = 0. The curve has a turning point here.

blocks.title: Differentiate f(x) = x³ − x²
blocks.goal: Use the difference rule and the power rule to find f’(x).

steps.label: Apply the difference rule
steps.explanation: The derivative of a difference is the difference of the derivatives.

steps.label: Apply the power rule to each term
steps.explanation: d/dx(x³) = 3x² and d/dx(x²) = 2x.

finalAnswer.summary: Differentiate each power term separately and keep the minus sign.

blocks.interpretation: At x = 1 the slope is 3(1) − 2(1) = 1; the curve is rising.

blocks.title: Differentiate f(x) = 3x⁴ + 2x³ − x
blocks.goal: Combine the constant multiple, sum, and difference rules with the power rule.

steps.label: Split into three derivatives
steps.explanation: Apply the sum and difference rules to break the expression into three pieces.

steps.label: Pull constants through
steps.explanation: The constant multiple rule lets each coefficient stay in front.

steps.label: Apply the power rule to each term
steps.explanation: d/dx(x⁴) = 4x³, d/dx(x³) = 3x², and d/dx(x) = 1.

steps.label: Simplify
steps.explanation: Multiply each constant by its coefficient.

finalAnswer.summary: Each term differentiates independently: bring down the exponent, multiply by the coefficient, reduce the power.

blocks.interpretation: A degree-4 polynomial differentiates to a degree-3 polynomial.

blocks.title: f(x) = x³ − 3x and its derivative f’(x) = 3x² − 3
blocks.description: The cubic y = x³ − 3x has turning points at x = −1 and x = 1. Its derivative y = 3x² − 3 is a parabola that crosses zero at those same x-values, confirming that the slope is zero at each turning point.

annotations.label: f’(−1) = 0
annotations.text: At x = −1, f’(−1) = 3(1) − 3 = 0. The curve has a local maximum.

annotations.label: f’(1) = 0
annotations.text: At x = 1, f’(1) = 3(1) − 3 = 0. The curve has a local minimum.

blocks.title: Applying the sum rule to a product
blocks.misconception: The derivative of f(x)·g(x) equals f’(x)·g’(x).
blocks.whyWrong: The sum rule says the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives. It does not say the derivative of a product is the product of the derivatives. The correct answer is d/dx(x⁵) = 5x⁴, not 6x³.
blocks.correction: Use the power rule directly: x² · x³ = x⁵, so d/dx(x⁵) = 5x⁴. A separate product rule exists for when you cannot simplify first.
blocks.checkPrompt: Compute d/dx(x² · x⁴) by simplifying first. Does the shortcut f’·g’ give the same answer?

blocks.title: Forgetting that a constant differentiates to zero
blocks.whyWrong: A constant has no x-dependence, so its rate of change is zero. The constant 5 vanishes when you differentiate.
blocks.correction: d/dx(x² + 5) = 2x + 0 = 2x. Every constant term becomes zero.
blocks.checkPrompt: What is d/dx(4x³ − 7)? Make sure the constant term disappears.

options.feedback: Correct. Apply the power rule to each term separately: 4x³ from x⁴ and 2x from x².
The second term x² differentiates to 2x, not x².
The sum rule gives the sum of the derivatives, not the product.
You cannot add the exponents and differentiate the result. Differentiate each term separately.

questions.hint: Differentiate x⁴ and x² separately, then add the results.

options.feedback: Correct. d/dx(3x³) = 3·3x² = 9x² and d/dx(5x) = 5.
d/dx(3x³) = 3 · 3x² = 9x², not 3x². Remember to keep the constant factor.
The derivative of 5x is 5, not 5x. The power rule gives d/dx(x¹) = 1.
The exponent on x must drop by 1. d/dx(x³) = 3x², not 3x³.

questions.hint: Use the constant multiple rule on each term, then the power rule.

prompt.value: . Write your answer as a polynomial in x.

feedback.incorrect: Apply each rule term by term: d/dx(2x⁵) = 2·5x⁴ = 10x⁴, d/dx(−x³) = −3x², and d/dx(4) = 0. The answer is 10x⁴ − 3x².

questions.hint: Differentiate each of the three terms separately. Remember that the constant 4 gives zero.

items.value: . A constant factor passes through the
. The difference rule is the same idea with a minus sign.
: split into terms, pull constants out, apply the
does not extend to products or quotients. Those need separate rules.

keyIdeas: d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f’(x) — constants pass through the derivative.
d/dx[f(x) + g(x)] = f’(x) + g’(x) — the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives.
d/dx[f(x) − g(x)] = f’(x) − g’(x) — the same rule works for differences.
To differentiate a polynomial: split into terms, pull constants out, power-rule each term, add.
Do not apply the sum/difference rule to products. d/dx(f·g) ≠ f’·g’.
recallPrompts: State the sum rule.
State the constant multiple rule.
What is d/dx(3x⁴ + 2x³ − x)?
Why does d/dx(f·g) ≠ f’·g’?

glossary.definition: The instantaneous rate of change of a function at a point, defined as the limit of the difference quotient.
The ratio (f(x + h) − f(x))/h. The derivative is the limit of this ratio as h approaches zero.
A line passing through two points on the graph of a function. Its slope is the average rate of change between those points.
The line through a single point of a curve whose slope equals the derivative of the function at that point.
The value a function approaches as the input approaches a target value. Written as lim_{x→a} f(x).
A function is continuous at x = a when lim_{x→a} f(x) exists, f(a) exists, and the two are equal.
A function is differentiable at x = a when its derivative exists at that point. Differentiability implies continuity, but not the reverse.
A function whose output never changes, written f(x) = c for some real number c.
The ratio of vertical change to horizontal change between two points; for a line, the rise over the run.
A rule that sends each allowed input to exactly one output.
The value supplied to a function, written as the argument in f(x).
The value produced by a function for a given input, written as f(x).
The set of inputs a function is allowed to accept.
The set of outputs a function actually produces over its domain.
The form f(x) used to name a function and show its input. Read aloud as "f of x".
A limit taken as the input approaches a target from only one side, written lim_{x→a^-} f(x) for the left side or lim_{x→a^+} f(x) for the right side.
A limit whose value is +∞ or −∞, recording that the outputs grow without bound as the input approaches the target. The symbol ∞ names a trend, not a real number.
A vertical line x = a near which the function's outputs grow without bound as x approaches a from at least one side.
A discontinuity at x = a where the left- and right-sided limits both exist but disagree, so the two-sided limit does not exist.
The differentiation shortcut d/dx(x^n) = n·x^(n−1). Derived from the binomial expansion of (x+h)^n in the first-principles definition.
The number that tells how many times a base is multiplied by itself. In x^n, n is the exponent.
A function built from sums of terms of the form a·x^n, where n is a non-negative integer and a is a real coefficient.
The derivative of a sum equals the sum of the derivatives: d/dx[f(x) + g(x)] = f’(x) + g’(x). The difference rule is the same idea with a minus sign.
The derivative of a constant times a function equals the constant times the derivative: d/dx[cf(x)] = c · f’(x). The constant factor passes through the derivative.
